examples.title: A* Graph Search Algorithm
examples.description: Complete A* search algorithm with OPEN and CLOSED lists for optimal path finding
examples.explanation: A* maintains two lists: OPEN (frontier) sorted by f(n) and CLOSED (explored). It always expands the node with lowest estimated total cost. For each successor, it checks if a better path exists. Graph search uses CLOSED to avoid re-expansion.

examples.title: Greedy Best-First Search
examples.description: Greedy search using only heuristic h(n) to select next node
examples.explanation: Greedy Best-First uses only h(n) (heuristic) to order the frontier. It always expands the node that appears closest to goal. Simple but not guaranteed to find optimal solution or complete.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b^m) where m is maximum depth

examples.title: Recursive Best-First Search (RBFS)
examples.description: Memory-bounded alternative to A* using recursion
examples.explanation: RBFS uses recursion and stack instead of keeping all nodes in memory. It stores only the path to current node and sibling information. When recursion returns, it updates f-values and backtracks if needed.

examples.title: Iterative Deepening A* (IDA*)
examples.description: Memory-efficient A* using iterative deepening with f-cost bound
examples.explanation: IDA* combines A* with iterative deepening. It uses f-cost (g+h) as the depth bound. Each iteration searches depth-first, incrementing the bound to the minimum f-cost that exceeded the previous bound. Uses O(bd) space like iterative deepening DFS.
